title: Mhencha
subtitle: Moroccan coiled filo pastry filled with almond paste and orange blossom water.
excerpt: Mhencha is a Moroccan celebration pastry: thin sheets of filo or warka pastry wrapped around a roll of almond paste, then coiled into a flat spiral and baked until golden. Sliced from the outside in like a wheel, it shows its coiled interior. The name means 'snake' in Arabic, which describes the shape before it is coiled. It is served at weddings, celebrations, and during Ramadan.
sourceNotes: Mhencha is a standard of the Moroccan pastry tradition, made across the country for celebrations and festivals. The pastry casing is traditionally warka (a Moroccan pastry thinner than filo), but commercial filo pastry works well in home kitchens. The almond filling is very similar to marzipan: ground almonds, icing sugar, orange blossom water, and an egg to bind. The snake shape (a long roll coiled flat) then dusted with icing sugar and cinnamon is consistent across regional variations. This recipe uses filo pastry throughout.

recipe.freezeNotes: Mhencha does not freeze well. Eat within two days of baking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The almond filling can be made two days ahead and refrigerated. Assemble and bake on the day.

recipeTools.notes: Large flat tray lined with baking paper.

content.text: The technique is simple but needs to be done quickly. Filo dries fast; keep the unused sheets covered with a damp tea towel while you work. You are building a long sausage of almond paste wrapped in filo, then coiling it flat onto the tray.
Work methodically: lay a sheet, brush with butter, lay another, add the almond roll, wrap, and roll forward. Each step is short. The whole construction takes about 20 minutes.

items.prepNote: kept covered with a damp cloth
for dusting, mixed with the icing sugar

content.text: Mix {{almond-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, {{orange-blossom-water}}, and {{cinnamon-ground}} together in a bowl. Add just enough beaten {{eggs}} to bring the mixture together into a firm paste that holds its shape when rolled. You may not need all the egg.
Roll the almond paste into a long sausage shape roughly 2 cm in diameter. It should be long enough to coil around a 25 cm tray. Set aside.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Line a large baking tray with baking paper.
Lay one sheet of {{filo-pastry}} on a clean surface with a long side facing you. Brush with melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Lay a second sheet on top and brush again.

Place the almond paste sausage along the long edge nearest you, leaving a 3 cm border. Fold the short side edges over the filling to enclose the ends, then roll the whole thing away from you to form a long cylinder. Brush the outside with butter.

Starting at one end, coil the cylinder into a flat spiral on the lined tray. Tuck the loose end underneath. Continue adding more buttered filo sheets and rolling the snake longer if you have remaining almond paste.
Brush the top of the coil generously with melted butter. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until golden all over.
Leave to cool for 10 minutes, then dust generously with the icing sugar and cinnamon mixture. Serve in wedges.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Filo handling is the skill to practise.

items.fix: Keep all unused sheets covered with a damp (not wet) cloth at all times. Work quickly. A few tears in inner layers are not visible in the finished pastry.
Fold the short side edges firmly over the filling and press them down before rolling. A small dab of water helps the filo stick.
